summary: I was born in Okinawa in 1902, and I have seen how Okinawa changed over the years. Before it became a prefecture in 1879, Okinawa was ruled by kings and had a unique land allocation system. Even after becoming a Japanese prefecture, Okinawa suffered from heavy taxation and social changes influenced by the Meiji Restoration and Satsuma Domain. My family and I experienced the traditional village life and the challenges of modernization in Okinawa.

The Meiji Restoration was a turning point for Okinawa and Japan as a whole. Around that time, clans were abolished and prefectures were established, which affected land ownership and social status. My grandfather renounced his samurai status to become a farmer during this period. The Restoration also brought changes in governance and taxation, although Okinawa's system took longer to reform fully.

Okinawa had a long history of trading with China, which was our main trading partner alongside Malaya and Thailand. China played a role in Okinawa's political history by supporting the Southern lord as king, which was a diplomatic move. Our leaders maintained relations with China, and through Okinawa, Satsuma Domain was able to trade indirectly with China during the Tokugawa period.

In the old days, Okinawa traded not only with China but also with Malaya and Thailand. These trading relationships were important for our economy and cultural exchange. However, China was the main partner among these, and trade with Malaya was less frequent but still notable.

Thailand was one of the countries Okinawa traded with in the past, along with China and Malaya. While China was our primary trading partner, we maintained connections with Thailand as part of our regional trade network. These exchanges helped shape Okinawa's culture and economy before it was unified under the Sho dynasty.

Tametomo Minamoto is an important figure in Okinawa's history. His son married the Southern lord's daughter, and through this union, Tametomo's son became king of the Central Tribe. This helped unify the three tribes of Okinawa under the Sho family, which has ruled ever since. The legacy of Tametomo's family still lives on in Okinawa today.

The Sho family has been ruling Okinawa since the unification of the three tribes, starting with King Shoen, who was originally a farmer from a northern island. The current Sho family is the third generation of this lineage. Okinawa had elections for kings, and extravagant kings could be defeated. The Sho dynasty represents a unique blend of leadership and commoner origins in Okinawa's history.

During the Tokugawa shogunate, the Satsuma clan governed Okinawa and used it as a gateway to trade with China and beyond, since the shogunate itself restricted foreign trade. Through Okinawa, Satsuma was able to bring goods from China and the South Seas back to Japan, enriching themselves. This period also saw restrictions on Okinawa, such as the prohibition of weapons after Hideyoshi's conquest.

The Satsuma Domain played a significant role in Okinawa's history by gradually conquering the Ryukyu Kingdom during Hideyoshi Toyotomi's time. Although they couldn't conquer Okinawa by force easily due to its distance and defenses, they managed to control it politically and economically. Satsuma used Okinawa as a trading hub with China and other countries, which was crucial since they were restricted from direct foreign trade under the Tokugawa shogunate.

The Ryukyu Kingdom was the original political entity in Okinawa before it became a Japanese prefecture. It was ruled by kings from the Sho family after unification of the three tribes. The kingdom had active trade relations with China, Malaya, and Thailand. Eventually, the Satsuma Domain conquered Ryukyu, and it became a tributary state, which influenced its governance and economy.

Toyotomi Hideyoshi was the Japanese leader during whose time the Satsuma Domain conquered Okinawa. He ordered Okinawa to send soldiers when he invaded Korea, but Okinawa refused, which gave him an excuse to conquer the island. After his conquest, Okinawans were forbidden from owning weapons, marking a significant change in the island's autonomy and defense.

Kagoshima was the place where I transferred ships when I traveled from Okinawa to Kobe to come to America. It took about 1.5 days to get from Okinawa to Kagoshima, and then 4 more days to Kobe. Kagoshima was also the home of the Satsuma Domain, which had a historical influence over Okinawa.

Nagasaki is known for its early Christian missionary presence, and I believe it influenced Okinawa as well. There is a place called Uragami in Nagasaki, which shares its name with places in Okinawa and Miyako Island, possibly named by missionaries. Although Christianity was prohibited except for the Shingon sect, Okinawa was still influenced by Christian ideas through missionaries.

In Okinawa, during the time missionaries tried to evangelize, all religions except the Shingon sect were prohibited. This made it difficult for missionaries to spread Christianity openly. Despite this, Okinawa was influenced by Christianity to some extent, but Shingon Buddhism remained the officially accepted religion.

My father was the first person from Okinawa to go to Imperial Valley in America, where he farmed starting around 1905. At first, he struggled for 2 to 3 years but eventually made a profit by leasing 40 acres with partners. Imperial Valley was a harsh place to live, with no houses and only tents in the desert, but water came from the Colorado River, so farming was possible.

Los Angeles was where my father moved to start a hog-raising business after farming in Imperial Valley. However, city regulations prevented hog raising near the city, forcing him to move the hogs back to Imperial Valley. Also, one of my father's partners, Mr. Ohta, opened Star Market in Los Angeles, becoming a successful businessman until evacuation during the war.

Fresno was a place where my uncle farmed and picked grapes during the harvest season. He would travel there every year and then stop in Los Angeles to buy Japanese food to send back to Brawley. Fresno was part of the agricultural circuit that many Okinawan immigrants, including my family, were involved in during the early 1900s.

Star Market in Los Angeles was opened by Mr. Ohta, one of my father's partners, along with Mr. Hirashiki. It became one of the most successful businesses from our prefecture before the war. Mr. Ohta managed the store until the evacuation, marking a significant achievement for Okinawan immigrants in America.

My father first went to Hawaii in 1904 before coming to America in 1905. Hawaii was a common destination for Okinawan immigrants before moving to the mainland. Although I did not go there myself, it was part of my father's migration path to establish himself in America.

I arrived in San Francisco in August, but unfortunately my father had passed away two months earlier in June. San Francisco was the port where I entered America after traveling from Okinawa through Kobe and Kagoshima. It was a significant moment for me as I came to join my father and settle in America.

I took a ship from Naha to Kobe as part of my journey to America. I could not get a passport until I arrived in Kobe, where the passport was issued after proper application and police approval. The trip from Okinawa to Kobe included a transfer at Kagoshima and took several days.

Naha was the port in Okinawa where I boarded the ship to Kobe on my way to America. It was the starting point of my immigration journey at age 16, just before I turned 17. Naha was an important hub for Okinawan travel and trade during my time.

The Colorado River was crucial for farming in Imperial Valley, where my father and other Okinawan immigrants worked. Despite the desert conditions, the river provided necessary water for agriculture, making the harsh environment livable. My uncle told me about this when preparing to come to America.

Argentina was mentioned as one of the countries connected through the South Seas trade routes used by the Satsuma clan via Okinawa. While I did not have direct experience with Argentina, it was part of the extensive trade network that influenced Okinawa's economy in the past.

Brazil was another country linked to the trade routes that passed through Okinawa under Satsuma's control. Although I never visited Brazil, it was part of the global trade connections that Okinawa was indirectly involved in during the Tokugawa period.

Peru was included among the countries reached by the Satsuma clan's trade via Okinawa. This shows how far-reaching the trade networks were, even though Okinawa itself was a small island kingdom. I know of Peru only through this historical trade context.
